description: Pharmacology grounding for the pharmacy/healthcare lens — pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and the safety principles that make drug reasoning sound. Educational reference content; not medical advice.

entries.title: Pharmacokinetics (ADME)
entries.summary: Pharmacokinetics is what the body does to a drug, summarized as ADME: Absorption (how much and how fast it enters the bloodstream — bioavailability F), Distribution (where it goes, the volume of distribution Vd), Metabolism (chemical transformation, mostly hepatic), and Elimination (renal/hepatic clearance). These four set the concentration-time curve that determines dose and dosing interval.

entries.title: Half-life & steady state
entries.summary: A drug's half-life t½ is the time for its plasma concentration to halve; for first-order kinetics it's constant (t½ = 0.693·Vd/CL). It takes ~4–5 half-lives to reach steady state on repeated dosing and ~4–5 to wash out. Half-life dictates dosing frequency: short half-life → frequent dosing or extended-release; long half-life → accumulation risk.

entries.title: Pharmacodynamics: receptors, agonists & antagonists
entries.summary: Pharmacodynamics is what the drug does to the body. Agonists bind a receptor and activate it (full or partial); antagonists bind and block it (competitive, surmountable by more agonist; or non-competitive, insurmountable). Affinity is how tightly it binds; efficacy is the response produced. The dose-response curve's EC50 and Emax quantify potency and maximal effect.

entries.title: Therapeutic index & narrow-margin drugs
entries.summary: The therapeutic index TI = TD50/ED50 (toxic dose vs effective dose in 50% of subjects) measures a drug's safety margin. Narrow-TI drugs (warfarin, digoxin, lithium, phenytoin, theophylline) need therapeutic drug monitoring because a small dose change crosses from ineffective to toxic. A wide TI tolerates dosing variability.

entries.title: CYP450 metabolism & drug interactions
entries.summary: Cytochrome-P450 enzymes (CYP3A4, 2D6, 2C9, …) metabolize most drugs. An inhibitor (e.g. grapefruit, ketoconazole) raises substrate levels toward toxicity; an inducer (rifampin, carbamazepine, St John's wort) lowers them toward failure. Most clinically dangerous drug–drug interactions are CYP-mediated, which is why interaction checking is core to safe dispensing.

entries.title: Renal & hepatic dose adjustment
entries.summary: Drugs cleared by the kidney need dose reduction when renal function falls (estimated by creatinine clearance / eGFR) or they accumulate to toxicity; hepatically cleared drugs need adjustment in liver disease. This is why renally-cleared, narrow-TI agents (many antibiotics, digoxin) are dose-checked against kidney function before dispensing.

entries.title: First-pass metabolism & routes of administration
entries.summary: Oral drugs pass through the gut wall and liver before reaching circulation; extensive first-pass metabolism can destroy most of a dose (low bioavailability), which is why some drugs are given sublingually, IV, or transdermally to bypass it. Route choice trades onset speed, bioavailability, and patient convenience.

entries.title: Major drug classes by mechanism
entries.summary: Drugs group by mechanism: beta-blockers (block β-adrenergic receptors → lower heart rate/BP), ACE inhibitors/ARBs (block the renin-angiotensin axis), statins (inhibit HMG-CoA reductase → lower LDL), SSRIs (block serotonin reuptake), NSAIDs (inhibit COX → less prostaglandin/inflammation), beta-lactam antibiotics (block cell-wall synthesis). Mechanism predicts both effect and characteristic adverse effects.

entries.title: Adverse effects, allergy & black-box warnings
entries.summary: Adverse drug reactions split into Type A (dose-related, predictable extensions of the drug's action — e.g. bleeding on anticoagulants) and Type B (idiosyncratic/immune — true allergy, anaphylaxis). Black-box warnings flag the most serious risks. Distinguishing a true allergy from a side effect or intolerance is critical to safe prescribing and substitution.

entries.title: Loading vs maintenance dose
entries.summary: A loading dose (≈ Vd × target concentration) rapidly fills the volume of distribution so effect starts without waiting 4–5 half-lives; the maintenance dose (≈ CL × target concentration) then replaces what's cleared to hold steady state. Loading is used when fast onset matters (antibiotics in sepsis, antiarrhythmics) and is independent of clearance — a key dosing distinction.
